media_name: Something That May Shock and Discredit You
mention_context: Daniel M. Lavery is the author of several books, including the newly published Something That May Shock and Discredit You. Tuck and Daniel discuss themes from the book, such as forced-masc content, gender euphoria, and writing about early transition.
Daniel M. Lavery is known as Dear Prudence and is the current host of the advice column and podcast at Slate.
Daniel M. Lavery is the cofounder of The Toast.
Tuck and Daniel discuss Mulan in the context of forced-masc content, and Tuck describes a song from the film as a '90s queer anthem'.
Daniel M. Lavery mentions She's the Man as an example where the 'awkward pleasure of the seen' in forced-masc fantasies appears.
Daniel M. Lavery references 'Duckie from Pretty in Pink is also a Beautiful Lesbian and I Can Prove it with the Intensity of My Feelings' as an example of his essay titles.
Daniel M. Lavery mentions that a deep knowledge of Mean Girls would be helpful for getting every possible morsel out of his book.
Daniel M. Lavery refers to Star Trek when discussing that readers do not need to be familiar with all his allusions to appreciate his book.
Daniel M. Lavery mentions that a deep knowledge of The Golden Girls would be helpful for getting every possible morsel out of his book.
Daniel M. Lavery uses The O.C. as an example of a show someone might have watched a lot to understand his book's references.
Tuck mentions hosting an episode of NPR’s Life Kit podcast, which is slated to come out in June for Pride Month.
The Heart is mentioned as the source of today's 'Theymail' message, an ad read on the show.
Daniel M. Lavery mentions having a fantastic interview with Chris Randle for Hazlitt.
Daniel M. Lavery refers to xoJane when discussing the impulse to 'just write whatever you feel' without editing.
Breakmaster Cylinder is credited for the podcast's theme song.
Blue Dot Sessions is credited for additional music in the episode, specifically the track 'Silent Flock'.
Ira M. Leigh is credited for the podcast's logo.
Daniel M. Lavery mentions Georgette Heyer as an author whose romance fiction often contains 'forced-masc' scenarios, presented under the guise of the 'great chaste lie of the plucky heroine'.
Daniel M. Lavery mentions Daphne du Maurier as an author of middlebrow literature that often contains 'forced-masc' scenarios.
They host the “Bad With Money” podcast, and in 2019 published a book of the same name.

Io is a cofounder of A.B.O. Comix, an abolitionist LGBTQ prisoner support network that helps incarcerated artists get their art into the world.
Amy Schneider won more than 22 consecutive games of Jeopardy!, becoming the first woman in the show’s history to do so.
The show notes link to New York Times articles about Amy Schneider making Jeopardy history and April Ashley passing away.
The show notes link to a PinkNews article about Lithuania's trans rights changes, mentioning they require a 'medical certificate of diagnosed transgenderism' for legal name changes.
Tuck discusses hearing recaps of the Sex and the City reboot, And Just Like That, noting its nonbinary character and Charlotte’s child coming out as trans.
Tuck mentions excellent recaps of 'And Just Like That' from Maddy Court in the Xena Worrier Princess newsletter.
A Theymail message promotes Panaphonica as lo-fi photography by a trans artist, where one can buy prints or enjoy photos.
Ira M. Leigh is credited for creating the podcast's logo.
Breakmaster Cylinder is credited for the podcast's theme music.
The podcast is introduced as a show by John and Kelsi, two crafty queer Canadians, who discuss real-life ramblings and fiber arts.
Tuck mentions that people might think 'be gay, do crime' has always been floating around in the atmosphere like carbon or Danny DeVito.
Io sells Dune fan merch and discusses the book series, including a debate about whether 'Dune is trans.'
Tuck confesses to never having seen Sex and the City, which explains why she isn't watching its reboot, 'And Just Like That.'
Tuck credits Maddy Court for 'truly excellent work' in recapping 'And Just Like That' in her Xena Worrier Princess newsletter.
Sara Ramirez plays a nonbinary character in 'And Just Like That' who co-hosts a podcast and later 'gay-ifies' Miranda.
An episode of 'And Just Like That,' in which Charlotte's child announces their new name via TikTok, was written by Samantha Irby.
Tuck references a good bit from an episode of 'Reservation Dogs' about a white guy questioning the 'landback' slogan.
Io recalls being in Marseille, France, and attending a Miyazaki movie fest with squatters before tagging 'be gay, do crime.'
Io mentions that later in the Dune series, Paul Atreides' son becomes the God Emperor of Dune by transforming into a sandworm and losing his genitals, which Io considers trans representation.
Tuck mentions famously interviewing Justin McElroy on this podcast for some reason, which delighted Io.
Io made a zine several years ago about anarchism and D&D, and they discuss how the game has become more mainstream and popular with radicals.
Io suggests 'The Adventure Zone' as one reason why D&D got back into the mainstream.
Io suggests 'Critical Role' as another reason why D&D got back into the mainstream.
Io names 'Rude Tales of Magic' as their personal favorite D&D-related show.
Tuck references a joke by Niko Stratis that she stole, about the 'transgenderist agenda.'

River Butcher clarifies that Che Diaz is a fictional character, meaning they don't have to compete with them, and also observes that audience reactions to mentions of the character are not always strong.
Moncho Ollin Alvarado is an award-winning poet whose book 'Greyhound Americans' was featured in a 'Theymail' message.

The poetry collection 'Greyhound Americans' by Moncho Ollin Alvarado is described as confronting a family history of borderland politics and discovering a legacy of violence, grief, trauma, and survival.
Breakmaster Cylinder is credited in the show notes for providing the podcast's theme music.

River Butcher mentions having appeared on 'Adam Ruins Everything' as an actor.
River Butcher mentions having appeared on 'Good Trouble' as an actor.

River Butcher is noted to have done standup comedy on 'Conan'.
River Butcher's new standup special, 'A Different Kind of Dude', premiered in January on Comedy Central's YouTube and they explicitly plug it at the end of the episode.
Tuck references talking to Avery from Girlpool, who transitioned to a man while being in a band with that name.
Tuck refers to River's early creative projects being built around being a lesbian and a wife on a show called 'Take My Wife'.
River hints that some material from Che Diaz's comedy might be lightly lifted from their 'Two Dope Queens set'.

media_name: Side Effects: On Being Trans and Feeling Bad
mention_context: Tuck mentions this book by Hil Malatino, noting that one chapter discusses finding ways to live in the 'in between time' of transition.
Amy Schneider is a famous Jeopardy! champion, and the podcast discusses her 40-game winning streak and experiences on the show. Amy also mentioned the difficulty of watching the show without a TV.
Ozzy mentioned reading a long biography of Franz Kafka and came away thinking he might have been a trans woman who didn't know it.
Diners is a guitar pop band whose new album, "Four Wheels and the Truth," was promoted via a "Theymail" message on the podcast.
This is the new album from the guitar pop band Diners, described as a rock-and-roll and sing-along record that addresses nonbinary realities.
Laverne Cox was mentioned in "This Week in Gender" as Mattel having made a Laverne Cox trans Barbie doll.
It was mentioned in "This Week in Gender" that Power Rangers created a they/them death ranger villain.
It was mentioned in "This Week in Gender" that Kendrick Lamar made a Macklemorian song about his trans family members on his new album.
Tuck spoke with Cassius Adair about the history of Transgender Studies Quarterly and trans studies as a field.
Amy Schneider made a podcast with friends about The Hunger Games, where they discussed the love triangle and theorized Katniss was clearly a lesbian.
Amy Schneider plans to include a section in her upcoming book about why Daria is the greatest animated show of all time.
Amy Schneider mentioned that her biggest claim to fame before Jeopardy! was a podcast about Downton Abbey.
Amy Schneider enjoys finding things like Moby Dick that she gets more excited about than most people and trying to convey that excitement.
Amy Schneider mentioned an article on Autostraddle about "the gay green couch," which resonated with her own desire for a similar couch.
Amy Schneider and her fiancée went through an intense "Girl in Red phase," referring to her as one of the "sad lesbians" she listens to.
Amy Schneider and her fiancée recently started listening to Caitlin Cobb-Vialet, whom Genevieve went to high school with.
Amy Schneider suggested Fiona Apple is one of the "sad women that I think ought to be lesbians" based on her songs about bad relationships with men.
Tuck references a quote from Amy Schneider in Allure magazine about the pressure of revealing more of herself with a positive public image.
